channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Starmer Resigns as Burnham Eyes Top Job
item.description: A decade after Brexit, the United Kingdom is still struggling to stabilize.

item.title: China Flexes Its Rare-Earth Muscle—Again
item.description: New export restrictions underscore Beijing’s supply chain grip in ongoing tensions with Washington.

item.title: Why Is Alibaba on a Pentagon Blacklist?
item.description: Washington doesn’t know how to untangle China’s military-civil fusion.

item.title: The Strange Logic of World Cup Ticket Prices
item.description: Aging stars and local fans dominate as Europeans stay home.

item.title: What Colombia’s rightward swing says about the country
item.description: Claire McQue, a Latin America contributor, reports from Bogotá on why voters embraced a tough-talking political outsider

item.title: How the Iran Deal Sets the Stage for More Conflict
item.description: As his Gaza agreement has shown, Trump is better at fanfare than follow-up.

item.title: The White House’s New Pro-Ukraine Tone Shift
item.description: Ukrainian gains on the battlefield may be propelling a mood change among top U.S. officials.

item.title: Russia’s war economy has problems—but is not about to crash
item.description: Vladimir Putin is still able to fund his aggression

item.title: Looking for a winner from the Iran war?
item.description: California’s pistachio farmers have gained, for now

item.title: How to turn compute into a financial asset
item.description: Entrepreneurs, exchange operators and AI firms are creating tradable instruments backed by processing power

item.title: China can be a useful bogeyman in American politics
item.description: Accusations of influence-peddling can muddy what is really going on

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: America’s AI power grab
item.description: How we chose this week’s image

item.title: Inside the Taliban’s New Military Formation on the Durand Line
item.description: Amid a simmering conflict with Pakistan, Taliban Supreme Leader Hibatullah Akhundzada approved the creation of a new 4,000-member military formation known as the Hebati Unit.

item.title: Trump’s March of Folly in Iran
item.description: In promoting his “deal” with Iran, US President Donald Trump has tried to dress up a humiliating defeat as a victory. While it’s tempting to blame this outcome on incompetent leadership, it stems more from the policies and institutions that have allowed entertainers and profiteers to rise to power.

item.title: Alan Greenspan’s Mixed Legacy
item.description: The longtime Federal Reserve chair inferred from past crises that lightly regulated markets, while prone to excesses, could right themselves sufficiently to avoid imperiling the financial system and the economy. The correct lesson would have been that markets require strict regulation, and that competent technocrats are essential.

item.title: Trump Tests the Limits of Farcepolitik
item.description: Will America join the long line of countries confronting defeat with self-delusion?

item.title: Kazakhstan Reverses Course on Direct Elections of District Akims
item.description: If earlier moves toward more direct elections were indictive of Kazakhstan's political maturation, what should we make of the reversal of that effort?

item.title: Will AI Yield Abundance Without Purpose?
item.description: Work and employment play a uniquely powerful role in structuring people’s days and conferring a sense of belonging, status, and self-worth. Although AI boosters insist that people would organically find new sources of meaning if most jobs are automated, there is no reason to assume so.

item.title: Tashiev Trial Closed Again
item.description: A week after being opened to public scrutiny, the Tashiev trial has again been closed once more.

item.title: To Avoid Climate Peril, the World Must Electrify
item.description: The war in the Gulf has unleashed powerful market forces that the rest of the world can harness to accelerate electrification and keep the Paris climate agreement's primary objectives on track. Doing so will not only mitigate climate change, but also strengthen economic resilience worldwide.

item.title: Dushanbe’s Long Arm Reaches Into Europe
item.description: A refugee in Vienna, a manufactured terrorism case, and a precedent Austria should not ignore.

item.title: Central Asia Is Becoming Europe’s Channel to Afghanistan
item.description: Central Asia and the EU can deepen cooperation on food, energy, and logistics to head off a humanitarian crisis in Afghanistan, with Kazakhstan on the front line as the region's grain powerhouse.

item.title: What the Iran War Taught the World About Food Security
item.description: Perhaps the most unsettling lesson from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is the gap between what we knew and how we prepared. Countries should use whatever easing of immediate pressures occurs to strengthen resilience before the next shock arrives.

item.title: The AI Dimension of India’s Act East Policy
item.description: For India’s AI vision to succeed, it will need to partner with East Asian countries. That makes AI diplomacy central to the AEP.

item.title: Alan Greenspan, One of the Most Influential Fed Chairs Ever, Dies at 100
item.description: A market maestro to some, he was criticized by others—including himself—for having too much faith in self-regulating markets.

item.title: Who’s Winning Southeast Asia’s De-risking Race?
item.description: As investment moves away from China, which countries are reaping the rewards?

item.title: Why Indian Women Are Being Driven to Suicide
item.description: Harassment for dowry and failure to produce male heirs are forcing women to take their lives.

item.title: Don’t Let China Stack the Deck
item.description: To combat Beijing, Washington needs a program to spread its AI worldwide.

item.title: The Orange Bandit
item.description: According to the Roman Emperor Tiberius, a ruler can either “shear the sheep” for many years, by encouraging productive activity from which to generate tax revenues, or he can “skin them alive” just once. By brazenly using the US government to pillage the country, President Donald Trump has apparently chosen the knife.

item.title: The Trump-loving right wins Colombia’s presidency
item.description: Abelardo de la Espriella promises an iron-fist and economic miracles

item.title: Strategic Affairs Expert Aleksei Zakharov on the Russia-Taliban Defense Deal
item.description: Besides “restoration of various Russian-made military equipment,” the Taliban regime may be keen on “Russian air defense systems” and “attack and surveillance drones” for use in conflicts with Pakistan.

item.title: Beijing’s Nepal Anxiety
item.description: Chinese analysts say that while the core concerns of a "close neighbor," i.e., China, are sidelined, Kathmandu is making every effort to accommodate the ‘"distant relative," i.e., the U.S.

item.title: The Obama Center Sets a Dangerous Precedent
item.description: Some of the basic functions of presidential libraries are fading.

item.title: China Could Win Taiwan Without Fighting
item.description: The cost of Trump’s equivocation.

item.title: The Only Way to Save Europe
item.description: The continent must act like a country.

item.title: When Workers Lose to AI
item.description: How the U.S. government can soften the blow of automation.

item.title: Targeting ‘Budget Efficiency,’ Indonesia Announces Changes to Free Meal Program
item.description: The scheme, which aims to provide free meals to 83 million children and pregnant women across the country, has faced scrutiny due to its high cost.
